title: Gulf Advisories Tighten Again, But Fares and Visas Get Easier
excerpt: US, UK, Canada and Australia raised Gulf travel warnings this week as hotel demand craters - pushing airlines and Saudi Arabia toward fare sales and easier visas.
content:
Two things are true about the Gulf right now, and they're pulling travelers in opposite directions. Western governments spent the past week tightening travel advisories across the region as the US-Iran conflict grinds on, while airlines and tourism authorities - facing a steep drop in demand - are simultaneously cutting fares and loosening visa rules to win travelers back. If you're weighing a Gulf trip for later this year, both halves of that picture matter.

On the advisory side, the moves have come fast. The US, UK and Canada updated their guidance for the UAE, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Bahrain, Oman, Qatar and Jordan around July 17-20, generally shifting to reconsider-travel or avoid-non-essential-travel language, with the US also pausing non-emergency visa services in parts of the region and the UK flagging a terminal closure at Kuwait International. Australia followed on July 21, raising Jordan, Oman and Saudi Arabia to Level 3 - reconsider your need to travel - which puts them alongside Bahrain, Israel, Kuwait, Qatar and the UAE at the same level on Smartraveller. Iran, Iraq, Lebanon, Syria and Yemen remain at the highest tier, do not travel. Separately, EASA is still telling European carriers to avoid Bahrain, Kuwait, Qatar, the UAE and part of the Gulf of Oman entirely, a bulletin currently running through July 29 and subject to renewal. None of this means airports are closing - Dubai, Doha, Abu Dhabi and Riyadh are all still operating scheduled service - but it does mean the official guidance from several major passport-issuing countries has moved firmly toward caution.

At the same time, the commercial picture tells a different story. UAE hotel occupancy fell about 16.5 percentage points to just 52.8% in June, according to CoStar data, and Saudi Arabia has seen a similar slide, as months of intermittent airspace disruption and conflict headlines keep leisure travelers away. Airlines and tourism bodies are responding the way any industry does when demand craters: with discounts. Etihad is running base-fare cuts of up to 20% on economy and business tickets across roughly 30-plus destinations including Zanzibar, Salalah, Kraków and Palma de Mallorca, for bookings made by July 26 and travel between August 1 and November 30 - though the discount applies to the base fare only, so check the full total against a non-sale search before assuming it's the better deal. Air Arabia has a separate one-way sale out of Sharjah starting around Dhs499, and Kuwait Airways paired 11 new summer destinations with a 15% discount on economy fares.

Saudi Arabia, meanwhile, is trying to make entry itself easier rather than just cheaper. Its new Package Visa Programme, rolled out through licensed agents Almosafer and Reserval, bundles a return flight, a mandatory four-star hotel stay and an e-visa with travel insurance into a single booking, with visa processing promised within 48 hours. It currently covers travelers from Jordan, Egypt, India, Indonesia, Pakistan, Bangladesh and Mexico, requires a minimum two-night stay and booking at least 48 hours ahead, and starts around 4,000 Saudi riyals per adult for the first two days.

So what should you actually do? If you have a trip to the Gulf planned in the next few weeks, treat the advisory upgrades as real signals, not background noise - check your own government's current guidance for your specific destination, since the picture varies country by country (Qatar and the UAE aren't rated the same as, say, Oman this week), and confirm your travel insurance explicitly covers conflict-related disruption before relying on it. If your trip is further out, in the August-to-November window the current sales target, the discounts are genuine and worth acting on if you're comfortable with the current risk level, but book with a fare or hotel rate that allows changes given how quickly these advisories have moved in either direction over the past few months. If you're eligible for Saudi Arabia's new package visa, remember the fine print - four-star hotel minimum, 48-hour advance booking, and it only works through the two approved agents for now, not a general e-visa replacement. And whichever hub you're routing through, recheck your specific flight 24-48 hours before departure, since the EASA airspace bulletin's July 29 review date is the next real point where this situation could shift again.
